text: Hello, I'm sharing a single port superpubic total abdominal colectomy with iliorectal anastomosis for colonic inertia. This is something that I've been doing over the last couple of years and I find it to be very doable, very reproducible, and very cosmetically pleasing for the patient.

Hello, I'm sharing a single port superpubic total abdominal colectomy with iliorectal anastomosis for colonic inertia. This is something that I've been doing over the last couple of years and I find it to be very doable, very reproducible, and very cosmetically pleasing for the patient.

The surgeon inserts a wound protector through a three-centimeter fan and steel incision using a single port access system. A 10mm, 30-degree bariatric length scope is utilized, allowing for hand spacing without angled instruments.

The surgeon inserts a wound protector through a three-centimeter fan and steel incision using a single port access system. A ten-millimeter, thirty-degree bariatric length scope is utilized for visualization. The surgeon operates with a Park's grasper in the left hand and a ligature in the right hand, using straight instruments to perform the procedure.

The surgeon holds a Park's grasper in the left hand and a ligature in the right hand, using these instruments to identify the ileocolic pedicle. The dissection proceeds from medial to lateral, entering the retroperitoneal plane.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle and performs a medial to lateral dissection, entering the retroperitoneal plane. A window is created around the ileocolic pedicle, which is then divided using the ligature.
The surgeon creates a window around the iliocolic pedicle and prepares to divide it using the ligature instrument, with minimal intra-abdominal fat visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully lifts the right colon mesentery away from the retroperitoneum using a single port access system. The ureter is visible in the surgical field, and the surgeon avoids high ligation due to the benign nature of the disease, focusing on taking the mesentery closer to the colon.

The surgeon carefully lifts the right colon mesentery from the retroperitoneum using a single port access system. The ureter is visible during this process, and the surgeon avoids high ligation, focusing on taking the mesentery closer to the colon due to the benign nature of the disease.

The surgeon adjusts the single port to change the instrument angles, facilitating the dissection. Using a 30-degree camera, the surgeon sweeps the duodenum down off the transverse colon mesentery, avoiding high ligations and employing a hand-over-hand technique.

The surgeon adjusts the port to change the instrument angles, facilitating the dissection. The duodenum is swept down, and the mesentery is taken using a 30-degree camera to view around the corner, separating the duodenum from the transverse colon mesentery.

The surgeon performs a hand-over-hand technique to continue sweeping the duodenum down off the mesentery, preparing to take the transverse colon mesentery. The inferior vena cava (IVC) and kidney are visible, with a notable absence of retroperitoneal fat. The right colic artery is identified, and the surgeon applies triple cautery to ensure secure hemostasis during the single port procedure.

The surgeon continues to sweep the duodenum down off the mesentery, preparing to take the transverse colon mesentery. The inferior vena cava (IVC) and kidney are visible, with a notable absence of retroperitoneal fat. The surgeon applies triple cautery to the right colic vessel, ensuring thorough hemostasis during the single port procedure.

The surgeon gently teases the duodenum down, working inferiorly just one centimeter above the pubic bone using a single port access system. The duodenum is being separated from the surrounding mesentery, facilitating the dissection process.

When I first started doing these cases, I thought of doing it because for all of my ulcerative colitis and Crohn's patients that I do subtotal colectomies with ileostomies, I always do it single port through the stoma site. The cases go rather nicely and I was always impressed that the angles, despite the location of the port, always seemed to work out. So I started to try it for other cases and from a different location. So obviously we're a lot more inferior here. We're really just one centimeter above the pubic bone.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the right branch of the middle colic artery, using straight instruments to approach the colon wall. The trajectory is adjusted after completing the medial dissection to the right colon and hepatic flexure. The surgeon then moves to the hepatic colic ligament, entering the dissection plane from below while staying close to the colon, ensuring the duodenum is safely swept down to avoid accidental damage.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the right branch of the middle colic artery, using straight instruments to approach the colon wall. The medial dissection of the right colon and hepatic flexure is completed. The surgeon then shifts focus to the hepatic colic ligament, entering the dissection plane from below.

The surgeon maintains close proximity to the colon while sweeping the duodenum downward, ensuring it is clear of the dissection area. The surgeon prepares to enter the dissection plane adjacent to the colon.

The surgeon navigates the hepatic flexure using traction with the left hand while hugging the bowel wall with a ligature. The lateral attachments to the hepatic flexure are taken down, and the dissection continues along the white line down to the cecum. The surgeon leaves the cecal and terminal ileum attachments intact to prevent the colon from twisting or obstructing the view, with the ureter visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates into the previously dissected medial plane, revealing visible bruising in the tissue. The hepatic flexure is approached, noting its relatively high position, yet manageable for the procedure.

The surgeon uses traction with the left hand and a ligature to hug the bowel wall, taking down the lateral attachments to the hepatic flexure. The dissection continues along the white line down to the cecum, with the ureter visible in the surgical field. The surgeon leaves the cecal and terminal ileum attachments intact to prevent the colon from twisting or flopping during patient position changes.

The surgeon proceeds to the next phase of the operation, taking the transverse colon mesentery along with the omentum. The transverse colon mesentery appears long, and the surgeon stays close to the bowel wall during dissection. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with the left shoulder elevated to enhance exposure as the surgeon approaches the flexure.

The surgeon carefully takes the omentum during the colectomy procedure, noting the ease of this step in thin patients. The omentum is separated from the surrounding structures, with the surgeon opting not to leave it behind.

The surgeon continues to dissect along the bowel wall, maintaining close proximity to the transverse colon mesentery. The camera is positioned suprapubically, providing clear visualization as the surgeon adjusts the patient's position to reverse Trendelenburg with the left shoulder elevated, enhancing exposure while approaching the flexure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the omental attachments from the colon near the flexure, followed by the mesenteric attachments to the same segment. The ligature is used to ensure thorough cauterization, preventing bleeding as the thicker tissues are separated.

The surgeon approaches the hepatic flexure, first taking the omental attachments to the colon separately from the mesentery. The omental attachments are removed, followed by the mesenteric attachments to the same segment, allowing the tissue to be pulled down sequentially. The ligature is used to ensure a full cycle of cauterization, preventing bleeding in the thicker tissues at this stage of the operation.

The surgeon uses a single port approach to carefully dissect the splenic flexure, removing attachments from the inferior border of the pancreas. The splenic flexure is nearly completely mobilized, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the descending colon mesentery while maintaining close proximity to the bowel wall.

I do do a lot of single port surgery. I think it is a nice tool to have in your repertoire, depending on the patient population. I do less single port surgery these days than I used to, but this type of case is something that I will always do single port. Again, a lot of it has to do with the patient and the body habitus, but I can tell you this always leads to very happy patients.

The surgeon carefully dissects the splenic flexure, removing attachments from the inferior border of the pancreas. The flexure is nearly completely mobilized. The surgeon then proceeds to dissect the descending colon mesentery, maintaining close proximity to the bowel wall. As the dissection progresses from superior to inferior, visualization becomes challenging, prompting a switch in approach.

The surgeon releases the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon, switching the patient's position to Trendelenburg to approach the dissection from the rectosigmoid area. The dissection progresses inferiorly, joining with the previously dissected area from the superior side, and continues to mobilize the mesentery.

The surgeon adjusts the patient back to the Trendelenburg position and begins dissection from the rectosigmoid junction, moving superiorly to connect with the previous dissection site. The lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon are released, and the surgeon continues mobilizing the mesentery, ensuring to keep a safe distance from the ureter.

The surgeon creates a window in the distal sigmoid colon mesentery, close to the bowel wall, to facilitate dissection. The lateral mobilization aids in easily accessing and taking the sigmoid mesentery. The superior hemorrhoidal artery is preserved. The surgeon plans to address the lateral attachments of the cecum, terminal ileum, and appendix last, ensuring the colon and small bowel do not twist. The cecum is prepared for exteriorization to divide the ileocolic junction, with the terminal ileum mesentery mobilized for better reach.

The surgeon creates a window in the distal sigmoid colon close to the bowel wall to access and dissect through the mesentery. The window allows for easier mobilization of the sigmoid mesentery, facilitated by previous lateral mobilization. The surgeon continues to dissect down to the rectosigmoid junction, preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the lateral attachments to the cecum, terminal ileum, and appendix, ensuring these structures do not interfere with the procedure. The cecum is exteriorized to facilitate division at the ileocolic junction, with the terminal ileum mesentery mobilized for optimal reach. The medial dissection is joined, and the colon is fully freed.

The surgeon removes the cap from the port and exteriorizes the cecum, using a Babcock clamp to grasp it. The ileocolic junction is exteriorized, and the mesentery is cleared further before dividing the junction with a stapler. A stitch is placed on the ileum. The divided colon is exteriorized back to the rectosigmoid junction, which is cleaned of its mesentery, and division occurs in the upper rectum through a low Pfannenstiel incision.

The surgeon removes the cap from the port and uses a Babcock clamp to exteriorize the cecum and ileocolic junction. After clearing additional mesentery, the ileocolic junction is divided with a stapler. A stitch is placed on the ileum.

The surgeon has exteriorized the divided colon back to the rectosigmoid junction, which is completely cleaned of its mesentery. The division occurs in the upper rectum, facilitated by a low fan and steel incision. The surgeon prepares for anastomosis by placing sutures to hold the ends and checks to ensure the mesentery is not twisted, positioning the small bowel to the patient's left.

The surgeon places the cap back on the port and aligns the small bowel to the patient's left, ensuring the mesentery of the ileum runs straight from the duodenum to the cut edge of the ileum without any twists. The ileum is repositioned to correct a twist, revealing the complete cut edge of the small bowel mesentery and the iliacolic pedicle, confirming proper alignment before proceeding with the anastomosis.

The surgeon inspects the mesentery to ensure there is no 360-degree twist before proceeding with the anastomosis. The ileum is observed to have spun around, indicating a twist, which is corrected by repositioning the loop of bowel under the mesentery. The cut edge of the small bowel mesentery is now visible, extending from the duodenum straight down, aligning with the ileocolic pedicle and rectum.

The surgeon completes a hand-sewn side-to-end anastomosis through the fan and steel incision, ensuring the mesentery is straight. The ileum is clamped, and the pelvis is flooded for a leak test, which is negative.

The surgeon completes a hand-sewn side-to-end anastomosis through the fan and steel incision, ensuring the mesentery is straight. The ilium is clamped, and the pelvis is flooded for a leak test, which is negative, confirming the integrity of the anastomosis.
